video_title: Too Early to Get Off the Wave | TCAF 248
gist: Carson Group's Ryan Detrick and Sonu Varghese argue the AI-driven bull market (8th longest since WWII, ~3.5 years old) has further to run, citing healthy breadth, broadening rotation into financials/industrials/small caps, and a real-economy capex story rather than a 2021-style bubble. The bigger risks they flag are memory-chip pricing power feeding into consumer inflation (Micron's margins = Apple's inflation) and a Fed that may have to hike rather than cut, plus a regime shift toward net-positive equity issuance as megacaps stop buybacks to fund data centers.

insights.claim: Detrick argues the current bull market, dated from the October 2022 low, is only the 8th longest since WWII at ~3.5 years versus an average prior length of ~7 years (shortest ~5), implying meaningful runway remains.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Provides a historical base-rate argument for staying long equities rather than de-risking on duration alone.
insights.quote: This bull market just cracked the eighth longest since World War II. The average length of those other seven was like seven years. The shortest was like five years.

insights.claim: The panel contends 2021 was the real bubble that already popped, with Apple down 30%, Google 44%, Amazon 56%, Nvidia 66%, Netflix 75%, Meta 77%, and ARK 80%, so current gains follow a completed boom-bust rather than precede one.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'are we in a bubble' question by arguing the cleansing drawdown is behind us, supporting continued exposure.
insights.quote: In 2021, Apple, the best of the bunch, fell 30%. Google 44. Amazon 56. Nvidia 66. Netflix 75. Meta 77. Arc the poster child 80.

insights.claim: Detrick notes that after Greenspan's December 1996 'irrational exuberance' warning the S&P 500 rose over 100% and the NASDAQ over 300% in the next three years, cautioning that overvaluation calls can precede years of further gains.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Warns investors against shorting or exiting purely on valuation/bubble narratives.
insights.quote: December 5th, 1996 was the famous irrational exuberance speech... the S&P 500 went up more than 100% the next three years. NASDAQ went up over 300%.

insights.claim: The panel argues the market will never again reach euphoric universal bullishness because media and social media guarantee persistent skepticism, so waiting for a 'everyone's all-in' signal to exit is a flawed strategy.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Removes a classic contrarian sell signal; sentiment will stay perpetually doubtful even in strong markets.
insights.quote: I don't think we're ever going back to that world where everybody is cheerleading... if that's what you're waiting for as your signal to okay, now everybody's in, it's over. It doesn't work that way anymore.

insights.claim: Detrick and Varghese explicitly say they have 'thrown the towel in' on timing a potential bubble top, choosing to ride momentum while scaling up diversifiers as it stretches.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Signals a barbell approach: stay invested in the trend but increase hedges/diversification rather than attempt to call the top.
insights.quote: we've sort of thrown the towel in on timing it. It could be a bubble. Maybe 5 years from now, who knows?

insights.claim: Over the prior 27 days the S&P 500's other 493 stocks outperformed the Mag 7 by 18%, the widest such spread since 2018, yet the index sits near all-time highs, which Brown calls extremely bullish.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Broadening leadership while the index holds up signals a durable, less concentration-dependent advance.
insights.quote: over the last 27 days, the 493 are outperforming the Mag 7 by 18%. That's a gigantic spread. In fact, it's the most that we've seen since 2018.

insights.claim: Brown notes Microsoft's relative performance versus the S&P 500 has round-tripped to 2019 levels, erasing seven years of outperformance, yet the broader market remains healthy.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Megacap leadership can fully unwind without breaking the bull if rotation absorbs it; watch relative-strength breakdowns in former leaders.
insights.quote: Microsoft relative to the spies is where it was in 2019. All of the outperformance in the last 7 years goodbye.

insights.claim: On a -1.4% S&P 500 day 285 of 500 stocks rose, one of only five times in 20+ years that more stocks advanced than declined on a >1% down day, evidence of healthy internal rotation away from tech.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Positive breadth divergence on down days suggests the selloff is rotational, not systemic.
insights.quote: On Tuesday, the S&P was down 1.4%. Yet 285 stocks in the S&P 500 were higher... Only five times going back over 20 years have we seen a day where the S&P was down more than 1%, but more stocks were up than down.

insights.claim: Detrick frames bull markets as cruise ships that are hard to stop once past their third year, applying the same logic to European equities that only broke out to new highs ~15 months ago after trading below 2007 levels.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Suggests both US and newly-breaking-out international markets have momentum runway, supporting global equity exposure.
insights.quote: Bull markets are hard to stop. They're hard to turn around. And that's like a cruise ship. Once they get past that third year, they tend to keep going.

insights.claim: Detrick argues rotation is the mechanism that keeps the bull alive, with financials and industrials (now at 52-week highs) replacing software as software stocks faded.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Sector rotation 'bails out' the index; investors should hold a barbell of tech plus cyclicals/financials.
insights.quote: just as one group loses favor, another group rises to take its place. So the software stocks had been a drag on the market, financials are making up for it and now they're all at 52 week highs.

insights.claim: Detrick says small caps are already leading on a relative basis with the Russell 2000 up ~20% YTD and the S&P 600 advance-decline line and price both hitting all-time highs, applying his 'breadth leads price' rule.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Small-cap breadth breakout, led by community/regional banks and biotech, points to where capital rotates if large-cap AI cools.
insights.quote: the S&P 600 advanced decline... closed an all-time high along with price. The way I learned it a long time ago, breath leads price.

insights.claim: Micron reported $41.5B revenue up 74% QoQ and 364% YoY, with 84-85% gross margins (vs ~$2B opex on $41B revenue) and forward 12-month earnings up 1440% since January 2025, a step-function the panel calls unprecedented.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Validates the AI-memory profit story but raises the question of how long such extreme margins and growth are sustainable.
insights.quote: last night they reported 41.5 billion of revenue up 74% quarter over quarter 364% year-over-year.

insights.claim: Brown's bear case is that Micron's earnings growth is a choke-point pricing phenomenon (60%+ ASP growth), not volume, with the company guiding tight DRAM/NAND supply-demand beyond calendar 2027.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: If gains are price-driven, the risk is customers engineering around the bottleneck or demand cracking, not a volume collapse.
insights.quote: Micron's incredible earnings and revenue growth are predominantly coming from them being a choke point and just raising prices. It's not volume. They're not making more. They can't.

insights.claim: The hyperscaler ecosystem (10-12 firms including Amazon-OpenAI deals) is funneling most of its forward revenue and cash flow to just two or three components suppliers in Taiwan and South Korea plus Micron, concentrating systemic dependency.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Highlights extreme supply-chain concentration risk where a handful of foreign component makers hold pricing leverage over the largest US firms.
insights.quote: you have the 10 biggest companies in the world saying here's all our revenue for the next three years... We're just going to give it to two or three companies in the in the Far East for components.

insights.claim: Varghese says S&P 500 momentum's excess return over the index is at the 95th percentile over 40 years and the 100th percentile over the last three years, with Micron alone now 12% of the S&P momentum index.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Momentum is historically stretched and Micron-concentrated; a reversal could be sharp, even if justified by earnings for now.
insights.quote: you look at the last 40 years it's at the 95th percentile last three years it's at the 100th percentile

insights.claim: Brown argues today's momentum leadership is unusually high-quality, comprising blue chips like Corning (150 years old) and Caterpillar (100-year Dow stock) rather than junky speculative names, justifying the stretch.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the assumption that stretched momentum equals fragile speculation; fundamentals back this factor run.
insights.quote: when people hear momentum, it has a negative connotation... and then you look and it's Corning which has been in business for 150 years... it's Caterpillar one of the top momentum stocks in the market.

insights.claim: Per the Bank of America Global Fund Manager Survey, 'long global semis' is by far the most crowded trade with nothing else close, more crowded than US growth stocks in 2020.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Extreme positioning crowding is the strongest single bubble warning the panel identifies; vulnerable to a sentiment reversal.
insights.quote: the most people have said long global semis are the most crowded trade. There's nothing else on this list that's even close.

insights.claim: Varghese says semiconductor multiples have de-coupled from their historic cyclical pattern (price-to-sales falling as margins peak), now expanding alongside all-time-high margins, implying either secular re-rating or exuberance.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: A key tell: if semis are still cyclical, today's multiple expansion at peak margins is a classic late-cycle warning.
insights.quote: historically semis were priced as a cyclical industry. As margins peaked, price to sales ratios would fall. Over the past year though, semi multiples have exploded as margins hit all-time highs.

insights.claim: Varghese finds the AI theme is so pervasive that over 40-50% of industrials constituents and many utilities are highly correlated to tech, while healthcare has near-zero correlation and looks strong.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Apparent diversification across sectors masks a single AI factor exposure; healthcare offers genuine uncorrelated diversification.
insights.quote: even for industrials, you know, more than 40% 50% have a large correlation with tech... Same thing with utilities... But then you get out into sectors like healthcare and there's zero.

insights.claim: Varghese sets a concrete falsification test for the AI trade: a sustained ~6-month rotation where tech stays flat while healthcare, financials, and comm services lead would signal the AI trade is done.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Gives investors a specific, time-bound signal to watch for the end of the AI leadership regime.
insights.quote: if you see a rotation for about 6 months tech is not doing well... and your healthcare comes back... I think then the AI trade is done

insights.claim: Detrick observes Nvidia and Alphabet are no longer working (Nvidia roughly sideways, up ~8-10%) while the momentum index is now driven by names like Lam Research, SanDisk, AMD, Teradyne and KLA.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Leadership has rotated within the AI complex toward semi-equipment and memory names; the original mega-cap leaders are stalling.
insights.quote: Nvidia is not working at all... it's sideways... You look at the momentum index it's Micron Alphabet... number five down the list is Lamb Research.

insights.claim: Brown highlights the launch of options on Roundhill's 2x leveraged DRAM ETF (RAM), atop the original DRAM ETF that grew to ~$20B AUM, framing it as a sign of speculative froth near a possible local top.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Proliferation of leveraged single-theme products and options on them is a froth/local-top warning sign in the memory trade.
insights.quote: You could you could buy calls on the 2X DRAM if we are not near some sort of a local top.

themes.theme: AI Capex as a Real-Economy Driver

insights.claim: Varghese argues IT equipment plus software now equals nearly 5% of GDP (up from ~3% a few years ago), and notably this excludes data-center construction, evidencing a genuine macro investment story behind AI.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Distinguishes this cycle from 2021 by anchoring it in real GDP-level investment; supports the non-bubble thesis but flags scale.
insights.quote: information, technology, equipment plus software... that's almost 5% of the economy, 5% of GDP. Few years back, it was about 3%.

insights.claim: Varghese stresses the rate of change matters more than the level: IT equipment/software as a share of GDP is now above the dotcom peak, and the slope of the increase (not the absolute level vs the 6.5% housing-bubble peak) is the key risk signal.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Frames how to monitor capex risk; a rapidly steepening slope toward the housing-bubble level would be the warning.
insights.quote: I think the green line going up as fast as it is right now, that's the key rather than the level of it.

insights.claim: Varghese predicts upcoming July earnings season will show hyperscalers spending even more on AI infrastructure (~2.5% of GDP), sustaining the capex-driven profit cycle.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Earnings-season capex guidance is the near-term catalyst that could extend the AI trade through H2.
insights.quote: once earnings season comes out, you know, during like July, these hyperscalers will tell us, 'Oh, we're spending even more on this stuff.'

insights.claim: Varghese notes emerging-market earnings forecasts have risen faster than US forecasts this year, driven almost entirely by South Korea and Taiwan (Samsung, TSMC) memory/AI exposure, while India is struggling.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: EM equity outperformance is really a concentrated AI-supply-chain bet, not a broad commodity/EM revival.
insights.quote: once you break it down, most of it is driven by South Korea and Taiwan. India struggling. We're getting a lot of lift from again the AI capex over stocks.

insights.claim: Varghese flags that US net equity issuance has turned positive for the first time in 26 years (excluding the 2021 IPO boom), reversing the long-running buyback-driven share-shrink regime.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: A structural shift from shrinking to growing share supply is a potential multi-year headwind to per-share returns.
insights.quote: for the first time in 26 years, well, 2021 was positive. That was the IPO boom.

insights.claim: Per Bloomberg, big-tech buybacks are disappearing across Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft and Amazon, with only Microsoft still buying back shares and that likely heading toward zero as cash is redirected to data centers.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Loss of the megacap buyback bid removes a major source of demand for megacap shares, a headwind to those stocks.
insights.quote: The only one that is buying back shares right now are Microsoft. And you got to figure that that's probably going to zero too.

insights.claim: Varghese reframes the 'cash on the sidelines' as corporate cash that megacaps previously used to buy back equity but are now spending on data centers, so the money flows into the real economy rather than supporting share prices.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Explains why issuance is rising and buybacks falling, and why this can be bullish for the economy but pressure megacap EPS/multiples.
insights.quote: The cash on the sidelines was cash with corporations... And what did they do with the cash? Bought equity. Now... that cash is no longer there. It's going to data centers.

insights.claim: The panel notes massive new equity supply incoming: Google raised ~$80B in equity, SpaceX raised ~$80B, and SK Hynix plans a ~$29-30B US listing, on top of pending OpenAI and Anthropic offerings.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: A wave of mega-issuance grows the available-share pie and competes for capital, potentially compressing multiples on incumbent names.
insights.quote: Google just had, you know, $80 billion... That same week that SpaceX came and raised they raised 80 billion in equities. SKHynix is going to do a $29 billion... reo here in the United States.

insights.claim: Brown notes the combined market cap of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic exceeds the total from the 1997-to-peak dot-com IPO mania, though Varghese stresses on inflation-adjusted proceeds-to-market-cap terms issuance is only ~one-tenth of the 1990s level (0.18% of S&P market cap YTD).
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Cautions against 'denominator blindness'; absolute IPO numbers look manic but normalized issuance is far below dot-com extremes, though approaching it if OpenAI/Anthropic list.
insights.quote: 0.18% of the S&P 500's market cap at the beginning of the year has come through new issuance... we are like one-tenth of where we were in the 90s mania.

insights.claim: Varghese argues Micron's margin expansion shows up directly as consumer inflation, evidenced by Apple falling ~6% in a day after warning of higher consumer-tech prices, with chip costs flowing through the PCE computer/software accessories index.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Links the AI-memory boom to a real inflation channel; one company's margin is another's cost, pressuring downstream hardware makers like Apple.
insights.quote: one company's margin expansion is somebody else's inflation.

insights.claim: Varghese says core PCE is still trending higher with core services ex-housing running hot at 0.5% in May (6% annualized) and ~4% annualized over three months, concluding the Fed has a genuine inflation problem.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Persistent sticky-services inflation argues against rate cuts and raises hike risk; key data for rate-sensitive positioning.
insights.quote: core services excluding housing is running hot... up in May 0.5% which is 6% annualized. Last 3 months core services outside of housing is running at 4% annualized. I think the Fed has an inflation problem.

insights.claim: Varghese expects the Fed to neither cut nor hike near-term, meaning policy effectively eases as inflation runs hot, telling investors to 'count the votes, not the dots' despite a hawkish dot plot.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: A 'run it hot' Fed is supportive for nominal growth and equities now, even as it lets inflation persist.
insights.quote: I don't think they're going to hike... which means policy is actually getting easier. They're basically going to run it hot. That's good for the economy.

insights.claim: Varghese warns the real danger is delayed: if inflation stays elevated into 2027 and the Fed falls behind the curve, a catch-up hiking scramble (like 2022) could kill the bull market, distinguishing a gradual hike pace from rapid 50-75bp moves.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: The pace of any tightening, not its existence, is the bull-killer; a 25bp July hike is survivable, a 2022-style scramble is not.
insights.quote: let's say you get into 2027, inflation's still elevated... 'We got a hike.' I think then it becomes a problem. I think then the Fed could potentially kill the bull market.

insights.claim: Detrick says Carson positioned portfolios for a higher-inflation world by adding managed futures (which have worked well after years of being a 'dirty word') and reducing bond exposure, arguing stocks historically do okay in higher-inflation regimes.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Concrete portfolio playbook for an inflationary-growth regime: own equities and managed futures, underweight duration.
insights.quote: we positioned our portfolios for a higher inflationary world. We added managed futures. That's been a very dirty word for a couple years... Managed futures done really well in this environment.

insights.claim: Detrick notes crude oil fell from $120 to $70, removing one urgent inflationary reason for the Fed to hike.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Falling energy prices reduce near-term headline inflation pressure and the case for aggressive Fed tightening.
insights.quote: Crude oil just fell from 120 to 70. So, if that was the urgent reason to hike, you don't have to worry about that anymore.

insights.claim: Varghese attributes the violent gold and silver unwind (gold cracking $4,000 and down since the war started) to rising real yields and a more hawkish-perceived Fed, with markets having priced out cuts and begun pricing hikes.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Rising real yields are the unifying driver pressuring gold; a key macro variable to watch for precious-metals and debasement trades.
insights.quote: I think that's a sign of real yields going up, the Fed becoming or perceived to be more hawkish... real yields going up that's hit gold.

insights.claim: Carson added a tactical gold allocation after the March 2023 regional bank crisis as a long-duration crisis hedge in lieu of long treasuries, and recently sold most of it tactically for the first time as real yields rose.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Demonstrates using gold as a non-duration crisis hedge and a timely tactical exit before its breakdown; gold remains a small strategic hold.
insights.quote: we added some gold... we did sell gold before it really started to break down.

insights.claim: Brown and Varghese argue Bitcoin has stopped trading like gold and now trades like a giant software stock (closely tracking Microsoft/IGV), explaining its January breakdown via the same rising-real-yields pressure hitting risk assets.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Reframes Bitcoin's correlation regime; treating it as software/risk-on rather than digital gold changes how to hedge or allocate it.
insights.quote: It's software. It literally it's code. It's trading like the biggest like a gigantic software stock.

insights.claim: Brown argues breaking-out regional and community bank stocks are a powerful bullish macro signal because these lenders depend on ordinary consumers paying bills (HELOCs, credit cards, auto and small-business loans) with no capital-markets cushion.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Strength in consumer-dependent banks makes a macro-doom case hard to sustain; a real-time read on consumer/credit health.
insights.quote: When these stocks are breaking out, it's very hard to make a macro doom case.

insights.claim: Detrick contrasts today with 2006-2007, when financials were breaking down and the advance-decline line had already rolled over before the GFC; today financials and smaller banks are strengthening rather than breaking down.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Healthy financials plus rising breadth is the opposite of the pre-GFC setup, arguing against an imminent systemic top.
insights.quote: we remember... 2006, 2007 financials were breaking down... and then everything fell apart with the GFC. Well, we're not seeing financials breaking down.

insights.claim: Detrick names credit spreads and advance-decline lines as his two primary tells, noting A-D lines are at all-time highs and IG/triple-B credit spreads show little fear, with new breadth highs historically preceding market peaks by ~11 months on average.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: These two indicators give an early-warning framework; their current health implies a market top may still be many months away.
insights.quote: You tend to hit new highs in market breath and the market peaks on average about 11 months later... if there's a monster under the bed... credit market's going to know it.

insights.claim: Varghese reports last-three-month nominal personal consumption ran at an 8.5% annualized pace (real ~2.1%), showing the consumer is still spending strongly even though much of the nominal figure is inflation.
insights.speaker: Sonu Varghese
insights.so_what: Robust nominal spending and a resilient labor market underpin the economy and consumer-dependent financials.
insights.quote: Last three months nominal consumption 8 and a half% annualized pace... Real is 2.1%... a lot of it is inflation but people are still spending.

insights.claim: The panel argues new Fed Chair Walsh is shifting toward a Greenspan-style opacity, citing a recent ~130-word statement (among the shortest outside crisis cuts) to preserve flexibility and avoid telegraphing moves in advance.
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: Less Fed transparency may not change the rate path but reduces forward guidance, a structural shift in how markets read policy.
insights.quote: I think he wants to preserve the ability to react to higher frequency data... he doesn't want everything to be telegraphed in advance.

insights.claim: Detrick disputes that less Fed transparency necessarily means more volatility (countering colleague Callie Cox's view), arguing volatility persists regardless because the Fed changes its mind so often and he prefers following price to Fed commentary.
insights.speaker: Ryan Detrick
insights.so_what: Suggests investors should weight market price action over Fed-speak given the Fed's mixed track record; volatility is endemic either way.
insights.quote: I'd much rather just follow what the market's doing than what some various Fed member is saying they think's going to happen when the Fed doesn't have the best track record.
